questions.prompt: What kind of charge was it?
Do any of these sound like your situation?
options: Non-conviction expungement under Ala. Code §§ 15-27-1(a) and 15-27-2(a)
Eligible conviction expungement under the REDEEMER Act
Pardoned felony expungement under Ala. Code § 15-27-2(c)
None of these / I am not sure

questions.helperText: jail/probation/parole, classes, community service, anything still owed

questions.prompt: Have you paid off everything the court charged?
Do you have any open cases right now?
questions.helperText: a charge in progress, or current probation/parole

questions.prompt: Did this happen because you were a victim of human trafficking?
Have you gotten a pardon or similar official relief for this?
When did the case end or finish?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What does the record say you were charged with?
questions.helperText: the charge name or code, however it's written

questions.prompt: Did the case involve any of these?
Do you have your court paperwork handy?
questions.helperText: No worries if not — you can save and come back.

questions.prompt: Where in Alabama did the case happen?

questions.helperText: also called a docket, cause, or arrest number — it's on your court paperwork

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma Internal use · not legal advice · verify current law How the agent should use this document Alabama routes by disposition and offense level, across two parallel statutes. (1) Non-conviction records are the most accessible: a charge dismissed WITH prejudice, no-billed by a grand jury, or resulting in not-guilty can be expunged after 90 days; a charge dismissed WITHOUT prejudice (and not refiled) after 1 year for a misdemeanor or 5 years for a felony; and charges resolved through a drug, mental-health, veterans, or deferred-prosecution program after successful completion. (2) Convictions (added by the 2021 REDEEMER Act): a nonviolent MISDEMEANOR/violation conviction after 3 years (§ 15-27-1(b)), and a PARDONED felony after 180 days (§ 15-27-2(c)). (3) Human-trafficking victims have a special, broader pathway.
Two statutes (misdemeanor § 15-27-1, felony § 15-27-2), each covering non-conviction records and a narrow
Non-conviction records (both statutes)
Conviction expungements (added by the 2021 REDEEMER Act)

Nonviolent MISDEMEANOR / violation / traffic / ordinance conviction (§ 15-27-1(b)): eligible 3 years after the date of conviction, with all probation/parole, fines, costs, and restitution completed, and the offense not excluded (not violent, sex, moral-turpitude, or serious-traffic).

PARDONED felony conviction (§ 15-27-2(c)): eligible 180 days after the certificate of pardon issues, where the conviction is not a violent offense (with limited exceptions) and not otherwise excluded.

Reclassified felony (Act 2015-185): a crime that was a felony at conviction but reclassified as a misdemeanor may be reachable where the person has been arrest-free (excluding minor traffic) for 15 years.
Human-trafficking victims (special pathway)

A person may expunge a misdemeanor or felony offense (even some otherwise-excluded ones) on proving by a preponderance of the evidence that they were a victim of human trafficking, committed the offense during that period, and would not have committed it but for being trafficked. This pathway is not subject to the usual per-person caps.
Route by disposition first: non-conviction (90 days, or 1/5 years for without-prejudice) vs. conviction
(nonviolent misdemeanor at 3 years; pardoned felony at 180 days). Then pick § 15-27-1 (misdemeanor)
Flag the trafficking-victim pathway when relevant — it can reach offenses that are otherwise excluded
and isn't subject to the normal caps.

Alabama expungements are filed in the criminal division of the circuit court of the county where the charges were filed, using the Administrative Office of Courts (AOC) expungement forms, with a mandatory ALEA-certified criminal history record attached.

Core components of an Alabama expungement filing 1 Petition for Expungement (the AOC expungement petition) — identifying the petitioner, the case (court, charge, statute, case/CC number), the disposition, and the statutory basis (§ 15-27-1 misdemeanor or § 15-27-2 felony, and the specific subdivision).
2 Sworn statement (§ 15-27-3) — the petitioner's sworn statement that the case is eligible and that they have no disqualifying pending charges or prior expungements beyond the caps.
3 Certified ALEA criminal history record — the petition must be accompanied by the official ALEA background check, which the petitioner requests and pays for in advance.
4 Filing fee or indigency declaration (§ 15-27-4) — the $500 administrative filing fee, or a request for a fee waiver based on indigency.
5 Proposed Order of Expungement (§ 15-27-6) — the order the judge signs directing ALEA and the agencies to expunge and archive the records.
AGENT GUIDANCE Use the AOC expungement petition for the correct statute (§ 15-27-1 vs.
§ 15-27-2) and attach the certified ALEA criminal history — a petition without the ALEA record is routinely rejected.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Statutory shorthand used throughout

Ala. Code § 15-27-1 Expunge a MISDEMEANOR / violation / traffic / ordinance — charges and (b) convictions

§ 15-27-2 Expunge a FELONY — non-conviction charges and (c) pardoned-felony convictions

§ 15-27-2.1 LIMITS on the number of expungements a person may be granted

§ 15-27-4 The administrative filing FEE and the indigency waiver

§ 15-27-6 The ORDER of expungement; certification; who may inspect expunged records

§ 12-25-32 Defines VIOLENT OFFENSE (excluded, with narrow exceptions)

§ 15-20A-5 Defines SEX OFFENSE (excluded)

§ 17-3-30.1 Defines crime of MORAL TURPITUDE (excluded)

ALEA Alabama State Law Enforcement Agency — holds the criminal history and the archive

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Two statutes (misdemeanor § 15-27-1, felony § 15-27-2), each covering non-conviction records and a narrow
band of convictions.

Non-conviction records (both statutes)
The original and most accessible relief — the same triggers appear in both § 15-27-1(a) (misdemeanor) and §
15-27-2(a) (felony):

• Dismissed WITH prejudice — eligible after more than 90 days.
• No-billed by a grand jury — eligible after more than 90 days.
• Found not guilty at trial — eligible after more than 90 days.
• Nolle prossed / dismissed WITHOUT prejudice and not refiled — after more than 1 year (misdemeanor)
or 5 years (felony), with no disqualifying conviction in the interim.
• Successful completion of a court program — drug court, mental-health court, veterans court, or a
court-approved deferred-prosecution / diversion program (petition generally 1 year after completion).
• Indictment quashed and similar dispositions.

Conviction expungements (added by the 2021 REDEEMER Act)
• Nonviolent MISDEMEANOR / violation / traffic / ordinance conviction (§ 15-27-1(b)): eligible 3 years
after the date of conviction, with all probation/parole, fines, costs, and restitution completed, and the offense
not excluded (not violent, sex, moral-turpitude, or serious-traffic).
• PARDONED felony conviction (§ 15-27-2(c)): eligible 180 days after the certificate of pardon issues, where
the conviction is not a violent offense (with limited exceptions) and not otherwise excluded.
• Reclassified felony (Act 2015-185): a crime that was a felony at conviction but reclassified as a
misdemeanor may be reachable where the person has been arrest-free (excluding minor traffic) for 15 years.

Human-trafficking victims (special pathway)
• A person may expunge a misdemeanor or felony offense (even some otherwise-excluded ones) on proving by
a preponderance of the evidence that they were a victim of human trafficking, committed the offense during
that period, and would not have committed it but for being trafficked. This pathway is not subject to the usual
per-person caps.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by disposition first: non-conviction (90 days, or 1/5 years for without-prejudice) vs. conviction
(nonviolent misdemeanor at 3 years; pardoned felony at 180 days). Then pick § 15-27-1 (misdemeanor)
or § 15-27-2 (felony).
Flag the trafficking-victim pathway when relevant — it can reach offenses that are otherwise excluded
and isn't subject to the normal caps.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Conviction waits run from the date of conviction (or, for a pardoned felony, from the pardon certificate);
non-conviction waits run from the disposition.

Disposition Statute Waiting period Note

Dismissed with prejudice 15-27-1/2(a) 90 days Both misd. and felony

No-billed by grand jury 15-27-1/2(a) 90 days —

Found not guilty 15-27-1/2(a) 90 days —

Dismissed w/o prejudice — misdemeanor 15-27-1(a) 1 year Not refiled; clean interim

Dismissed w/o prejudice — felony 15-27-2(a) 5 years Not refiled; clean interim

Court-program completion 15-27-1/2(a) ~1 year after completion Drug/MH/veterans/deferred

Nonviolent misdemeanor CONVICTION 15-27-1(b) 3 years From date of conviction

PARDONED felony conviction 15-27-2(c) 180 days From pardon certificate

Reclassified felony (Act 2015-185) 15-27-1/2 15 years arrest-free Narrow pathway

Across all conviction pathways, the petitioner must first have completed every term of the sentence — all
probation or parole, and payment of all fines, court costs, restitution, and other court-ordered amounts — before
the waiting clock is treated as satisfied (the indigency provisions of § 15-27-4 can affect the fee, but not the
requirement that ordered amounts be resolved). For non-conviction “without prejudice” dispositions, the person
must also have stayed free of disqualifying convictions during the 1-year (misdemeanor) or 5-year (felony)
window and the charge must not have been refiled.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Anchor conviction waits correctly: 3 years from the conviction date for a nonviolent misdemeanor, 180
days from the pardon for a pardoned felony. Confirm all fines/costs/restitution are paid before counting
the wait as met.
For a charge dismissed without prejudice, the clock is 1 year (misdemeanor) or 5 years (felony), and only
if it wasn't refiled and the person stayed conviction-free.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / excluded offenses
sourceSections.text:
For CONVICTION expungements, eligibility turns on the offense not falling into an excluded category. The
exclusions are broad.

Categorically excluded (conviction expungements)
• Violent offenses as defined in § 12-25-32 — excluded (with a narrow exception for certain offenses
committed as a trafficking victim).
• Sex offenses as defined in § 15-20A-5 — excluded (trafficking-victim exception aside).
• Crimes involving moral turpitude as defined in § 17-3-30.1 — excluded for misdemeanor-conviction
expungement (subject to the reclassified-felony 15-year provision).
• Serious traffic offenses under Article 9 of Chapter 5A of Title 32 — including DUI — excluded.
• Commercial-driver offenses (49 C.F.R. § 383.51) where the person held a CDL/CLP or was operating a
commercial vehicle at the time — excluded.

Limits on the number of expungements (§ 15-27-2.1)
• Unlimited expungements for most NON-conviction dispositions (the (a)(1)–(a)(5), (a)(7), (a)(8) triggers in
both statutes, and § 15-27-2(b)).
• One expungement for a pardoned-felony conviction (§ 15-27-2(c)).
• Two expungements for misdemeanor convictions and for court-program-completion dispositions (the (a)(6)
and § 15-27-1(b) categories).
• One expungement = all charges from the same arrest or incident, so a single multi-count arrest counts
once against the cap.

Other prerequisites and limits
• All court-ordered amounts paid (fines, costs, restitution) and probation/parole completed.
• No pending charges and (for without-prejudice dismissals) a clean interim record.
• Petition-only. Alabama has no automatic expungement — every record requires a filed petition.
• Out-of-state / federal convictions are not reachable by Alabama expungement.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The excluded categories are defined by cross-referenced statutes (§ 12-25-32 violent, § 15-20A-5
sex, § 17-3-30.1 moral turpitude, Title 32 serious-traffic) and the § 15-27-2.1 caps are intricate and
were set by the 2021 REDEEMER Act. The agent must map each offense to its Alabama
classification, screen against the exclusion lists, and check the per-person cap before declaring
eligibility — and verify current text, since these provisions have been amended repeatedly.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

AGENT GUIDANCE
Screen conviction petitions against the four exclusions first: violent (§ 12-25-32), sex (§ 15-20A-5), moral
turpitude (§ 17-3-30.1), and serious-traffic/DUI. Any of these defeats a conviction expungement.
Track the § 15-27-2.1 caps: two misdemeanor-conviction expungements, one pardoned felony, but
unlimited for most non-conviction records. Count a multi-count arrest as one.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Disposition and offense level drive the statute, the wait, and the per-person cap. This table is the agent's primary
routing logic for Alabama.

Case outcome Statute Wait Cap (§ 15-27-2.1)

Dismissed with prejudice / no-bill / 15-27-1/2(a) 90 days Unlimited
not guilty

Dismissed w/o prejudice — misd. 15-27-1(a) 1 year Unlimited

Dismissed w/o prejudice — felony 15-27-2(a) 5 years Unlimited

Court-program completion 15-27-1/2(a)(6) ~1 year Two

Nonviolent misdemeanor conviction 15-27-1(b) 3 years Two

Pardoned felony conviction 15-27-2(c) 180 days One

Trafficking-victim offense 15-27-1/2(a)(8) On proof Not capped

Violent / sex / moral-turpitude / — Not eligible —
serious-traffic conviction

Effect of relief (§ 15-27-6): once an expungement order is granted, the proceedings are deemed never to have
occurred, and the person may lawfully deny or fail to acknowledge the arrest, charge, or conviction in most
contexts (with statutory exceptions, e.g., applications to law enforcement and certain licensing or when
testifying). The records are removed from public access and the offense is withdrawn from the national
repository, but ALEA ARCHIVES the records and law enforcement, prosecutors, and certain agencies retain
limited access (§§ 15-27-7, 15-27-8). The relief reaches Alabama records only.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tell users the expungement is strong in everyday life — the matter is deemed never to have occurred
and drops off public background checks — but ALEA keeps an archived copy and law enforcement and
some agencies still see it.
Match the statute, wait, and cap together: e.g., a nonviolent misdemeanor conviction is § 15-27-1(b),
3-year wait, and counts against the two-expungement cap.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Alabama expungements are filed in the criminal division of the circuit court of the county where the charges
were filed, using the Administrative Office of Courts (AOC) expungement forms, with a mandatory
ALEA-certified criminal history record attached.

Core components of an Alabama expungement filing
1 Petition for Expungement (the AOC expungement petition) — identifying the petitioner, the case (court,
charge, statute, case/CC number), the disposition, and the statutory basis (§ 15-27-1 misdemeanor or §
15-27-2 felony, and the specific subdivision).

2 Sworn statement (§ 15-27-3) — the petitioner's sworn statement that the case is eligible and that they have
no disqualifying pending charges or prior expungements beyond the caps.
3 Certified ALEA criminal history record — the petition must be accompanied by the official ALEA
background check, which the petitioner requests and pays for in advance.

4 Filing fee or indigency declaration (§ 15-27-4) — the $500 administrative filing fee, or a request for a fee
waiver based on indigency.

5 Proposed Order of Expungement (§ 15-27-6) — the order the judge signs directing ALEA and the agencies
to expunge and archive the records.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Use the AOC expungement petition for the correct statute (§ 15-27-1 vs. § 15-27-2) and attach the
certified ALEA criminal history — a petition without the ALEA record is routinely rejected.
Pair the petition with the sworn statement and the proposed order; have the $500 fee or an indigency
declaration ready.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Request the official ALEA criminal history record (and the court file) to confirm each
charge, the disposition, the offense classification, and the conviction or completion dates.

2 Confirm the statute and eligibility. Non-conviction vs. conviction; § 15-27-1 (misdemeanor) vs. § 15-27-2
(felony); the waiting period; the § 15-27-2.1 cap; that all fines/costs/restitution are paid and probation/parole
completed; and that the offense is not excluded.

3 Prepare the petition, sworn statement, and proposed order citing the exact statute and subdivision, and
attach the certified ALEA criminal history record.

4 File in the criminal division of the circuit court of the county where the charges were filed, and pay the
$500 administrative filing fee (§ 15-27-4) or file an indigency declaration for a waiver.
5 Service (§ 15-27-3). Serve the district attorney and the law-enforcement / arresting agency and (where
applicable) the victim; they have a window to object.

6 Objection / hearing (§ 15-27-5). If the DA, a victim, or law enforcement objects within the statutory period,
the court holds a hearing; if there is no objection, the court may rule on the petition without a hearing.

7 Order and archive (§§ 15-27-6 to 15-27-8). If granted, the court enters the Order of Expungement; the
records are withdrawn from the national repository and public access, and ALEA archives them. Keep a
certified copy of the order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is the circuit court of the county where the charges were FILED. The certified ALEA criminal
history must be attached up front, and the $500 fee (or an indigency declaration) is required per petition.
Build in service on the DA and arresting agency — their objection window drives whether a hearing is
set.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• Petition-only, no automatic relief. Every Alabama expungement requires a filed petition; nothing clears by
operation of law.
• Two statutes. § 15-27-1 governs misdemeanors/violations/traffic/ordinances; § 15-27-2 governs felonies.
Pick the right one and the right subdivision.
• 2021 REDEEMER Act. For the first time, certain CONVICTIONS can be expunged — nonviolent
misdemeanors (3 years) and pardoned felonies (180 days). Older guides predating July 2021 are unreliable.
• High fee. The administrative filing fee is $500 per petition (indigency waiver available) — among the most
expensive in the country.
• Per-person caps (§ 15-27-2.1). Unlimited for most non-conviction records, two for misdemeanor
convictions, one for a pardoned felony; one expungement covers all charges from the same arrest.
• Certified ALEA record required. The petition must include the official ALEA criminal history.
• Broad exclusions. Violent (§ 12-25-32), sex (§ 15-20A-5), moral-turpitude (§ 17-3-30.1), and
serious-traffic/DUI offenses cannot be expunged as convictions.
• Records archived, not destroyed. ALEA keeps an archive and law enforcement retains limited access even
after expungement.
• Trafficking-victim pathway can reach otherwise-excluded offenses and is not subject to the caps.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the cost and the cap: the $500 fee per petition and the limited number of conviction
expungements mean petitioners should prioritize which records to clear.
Confirm the matter is post-July-2021 framing — conviction expungement didn't exist before the
REDEEMER Act, so a user's older understanding may be wrong.

Alabama Expungement — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

Example A — Petition to Expunge a Nonviolent Misdemeanor Conviction (§ 15-27-1(b))

CRIMINAL DIVISION
IN RE: PETITION OF ANDRE L. CARTER Case No. CC-2019-001234

PETITION FOR EXPUNGEMENT (Ala. Code § 15-27-1(b))
1. Petitioner: Andre L. Carter; DOB 07/22/1992.
2. On 03/14/2019, in this county, petitioner was convicted of
Theft of Property 3rd (a Class A misdemeanor), § 13A-8-5.

them; under the statute the proceedings are deemed never to have occurred, and the petitioner may lawfully
